confirmed_defects.title: menu-snapshot wrapper-dict permanently disables the per-field probe fallback for prompt_log and workflows
confirmed_defects.defect: cmd_menu_snapshot builds each section as a wrapper dict via `{k: (probe or {}).get(k) for k in (...)}` (lines 230/233/240). When an underlying probe subprocess FAILS, `_tool_json` returns None, so the field becomes a NON-NULL dict with null inner values, e.g. prompt_log -> {"enabled":null,"should_prompt":null}, workflows -> {"total":null,...}. The menu's fallback for these fields is on the WHOLE wrapper: menu.md line 191 `plog-state <- snap.prompt_log | TOOL(prompt-log, consent)` and line 279 `wf-list <- snap.workflows | TOOL(workflow-runner, list, ...)`. Since the wrapper dict is always truthy, the `|` fallback NEVER fires; the menu reads null inner values instead of the real probe values (consent banner mis-renders; workflow line shows 'None installed'). Contrast the fields that fall back on a LEAF — `snap.drift.state` (line 186) and `snap.dispatch_index_count` (line 61) — which are null on failure and DO fall back. The equivalence test only exercises the happy path (probes succeed) and never simulates a probe failure, so the dead-fallback is uncaught. This breaks the stated per-field-fallback parity for prompt_log and workflows.
confirmed_defects.fix:
In tools/axon_state.py, emit None for the whole field when the underlying probe failed so the wrapper-access `|` fallback fires like the leaf fields:

L233: out["prompt_log"] = {k: plog.get(k) for k in ("enabled", "should_prompt")} if plog else None
L240: out["workflows"]  = {k: wf.get(k)   for k in ("total", "reference", "user")} if wf  else None

Verified: happy path yields the identical wrapper dict (equivalence test still passes); failure path yields None/∅ so menu.md L191/L279 fall back to the real probe, restoring parity with the leaf-fallback fields.

confirmed_defects.title: boot --brief drops tools.names, but the kernel boot procedure sources W:tool-registry from it — emptying the registry and breaking membership-gated consumers
confirmed_defects.defect: BRIEF emits tools as `{"count": len(tools)}` with NO `names` (149 names dropped). But axon/BOOT.md STEP 2 line 59 sets `boot-result.tools.names -> W:tool-registry`, and grep confirms W:tool-registry has no other population source. In brief mode boot-result.tools.names is undefined, so W:tool-registry becomes empty. Real consumers need the names/objects, not a count: (a) axon/programs/mode-system.md:8-13 does FIND(tools, status=ACTIVE/FAILED/SKIPPED) — collapses to 0 with an empty registry; (b) axon/programs/PROGRAMS-SLIM.md:9 `ASSERT(tools declared ∈ W:tool-registry)` — membership check fails for every tool; (c) axon/BOOT.md:92 `IF "cron" in W:tool-registry` guards the boot-time cron-overdue check — false on empty registry, so overdue scheduled programs are silently never surfaced; (d) list-tools.md:20, harness-builder.md:66/96 render/iterate the names. The menu only needs COUNT(W:tool-registry), but these other consumers need the list, so the claim 'only counts/ids needed' is false. (The cron.overdue-object drop, by contrast, holds: BOOT.md gets labels via an independent TOOL(cron, check), and boot.py's own surfacing only uses overdue_ids/overdue_count.)
confirmed_defects.fix:
Restore the load-bearing field in the BRIEF envelope while keeping the token win elsewhere. In tools/boot.py, change the brief branch so `tools_out` still carries the name list (it is the source of truth for W:tool-registry membership and COUNT). Minimal edit at line 359:

    else:
        # BRIEF (default): keep tool names — W:tool-registry is sourced from
        # tools.names (axon/BOOT.md:59) and consumers do COUNT/membership on it.
        tools_out = {"count": len(tools), "names": tools}
        cron_out = { ... }  # unchanged — cron's heavy `overdue` objects ARE unused, that drop is safe

Then update test_integration.py:44 (test_brief_default_is_compact) which currently pins `"names" not in out["tools"]` — that assertion encodes the bug; relax it to assert names IS present in brief (or drop that one line) so the suite reflects the corrected contract.

Alternative (preserves pr-8's exact token target but more spec churn): keep brief without names but (a) store `boot-result.tools.count` into W:tool-registry-count and re-point menu.md:26/BOOT.md:166 to it, and (b) re-point BOOT.md:59 + the membership consumers (BOOT.md:92, PROGRAMS-SLIM.md:9, mode-system.md:8) to read names from --full / REGISTRY.json directly. The first fix is smaller and correct; prefer it.

confirmed_defects.title: define --force fully bypasses the budget wall AND terminal-state refusal — no human token required
confirmed_defects.defect: define() (lines 84-107) accepts --force with NO human-confirm token. On an existing loop it overwrites status back to RUNNING, raises budget to any value, and zeroes out the iterations list. rebudget()'s human gate (line 166) and the terminal-state refusals in iterate() (line 114) and replan() (line 153) are all rendered meaningless: an autonomous agent reaches the same outcome rebudget forbids by calling `define --id X ... --budget 999 --force`. This is not a hypothetical surface — loop-designer.md line 73-75 already uses `TOOL(loop-contract, define, "... --force")` as a sanctioned autonomous idiom, and per the harness binding TOOL(loop-contract, define, ...) maps directly to the CLI with arbitrary flags, so the autonomous loop machinery can emit --force.

confirmed_defects.title: Ramp authorization state (quality.ramp.json) is unsigned, agent-writable, and self-asserted — no integrity boundary between the loop and the gate that authorizes it
confirmed_defects.defect: All ramp state lives in plain JSON at workspace/loops/quality.ramp.json which the autonomous loop fully controls, and ramp_status() reads cycles[].clean straight from that file with no provenance, signature, or independent verification. The loop authors its own clean records via ramp-record (quality-loop.md line 66 — the loop's own self-assessment), and any process able to write the workspace can append three {clean:true} entries to grant itself s_fix_authorized=true, or set locked=false directly, never touching ramp-unlock's --human-confirm gate. The human-only unlock wall on ramp_unlock() is bypassable by writing the JSON, just as rebudget is bypassable by define --force. The authorization is self-attested, not independently witnessed.

confirmed_defects.title: R_TOOL_RECEIPTS: 'verify' in _EXEMPT is an unconditional mimicry bypass — a fabricated verify/gate-pass is never blocked
confirmed_defects.defect: _EXEMPT = {'name','tool','x','verify'} subtracts 'verify' from the claimed set BEFORE the receipt check (line 36, line 63). The comment justifies it as 'verify runs THIS check — can't receipt itself mid-turn', but the exemption is unconditional: ANY claim of the form TOOL(verify, ...) is silently dropped, receipted or not. A weak model fabricating a passing gate ('TOOL(verify, run) -> gate GREEN, all pass') is exactly the mimicry the rule exists to catch, and it sails through. 'tool' and 'name' are similarly exploitable (TOOL(tool, dispatch), TOOL(name, ...)).
confirmed_defects.fix:
In /home/arturcastiel/projects/new-axon/axon/tools/rules/r_tool_receipts.py line 36, remove the un-receiptable-by-name exemptions. Change:
  _EXEMPT = {"name", "tool", "x", "verify"}  # verify runs THIS check — can't receipt itself mid-turn
to:
  _EXEMPT = {"x"}  # 'x' is the doc/placeholder meta-token; all real tools (incl. verify) are receipted by axon.py dispatch
'verify' is an ACTIVE tool the dispatcher receipts on every real run, so an honest run produces a receipt and passes; only a fabricated claim with no receipt is blocked — which is the rule's whole purpose. 'name'/'tool' are not tools and must not be a free pass. If a real false-positive ever arises from the Stop hook's verify-output self-check legitimately narrating the in-flight verify run, guard ONLY that one self-referential case narrowly (e.g. suppress a verify claim solely when the verifier itself is the invoker) rather than blanket-dropping the token from the claimed set.

confirmed_defects.title: R_TOOL_RECEIPTS: regex only matches literal 'TOOL(' — the real dispatcher CLI form and all prose/spaced variants are invisible (mass false negative)
confirmed_defects.defect: _CLAIM_RE = r'\bTOOL\(\s*([a-z0-9][\w-]*)' (line 33) only fires on the literal token 'TOOL(' with no space before the paren. A model that narrates execution in any other surface form is never inspected: (a) the actual dispatcher invocation form `python3 axon.py drift gate` (the exact form receipts are generated FOR) -> 0 matches; (b) prose 'I invoked the clock tool and it returned ...' -> 0 matches; (c) 'TOOL (clock)' with a space, 'TOOL: clock', or 'TOOL clock' -> 0 matches; (d) a name with a non-alnum first char 'TOOL(.secret-exfil)' -> 0 matches. So the easiest, most natural way to fabricate a tool result (describe the CLI command and paste output) is entirely unchecked. Compounding this: any honest output that DOES use TOOL( trips R7 ('translate before emitting', a WARN), nudging models toward exactly the prose form that evades this rule.
confirmed_defects.fix:
In /home/arturcastiel/projects/new-axon/axon/tools/rules/r_tool_receipts.py, broaden the claim detector to cover the dispatcher CLI form (the canonical receipt-writing path) and tolerate whitespace before the paren, then union both capture groups. Replace line 33:

  _CLAIM_RE = re.compile(r"\bTOOL\(\s*([a-z0-9][\w-]*)", re.IGNORECASE)

with:

  _CLAIM_RE = re.compile(
      r"\bTOOL\s*\(\s*([a-z0-9][\w-]*)"   # symbolic TOOL( name  (now space-tolerant)
      r"|axon\.py\s+([a-z0-9][\w-]*)",     # dispatcher CLI: python3 axon.py <tool>
      re.IGNORECASE)

and update the comprehension on line 63 to read whichever group matched:

  claimed = {(m.group(1) or m.group(2)).lower() for m in _CLAIM_RE.finditer(text)} - _EXEMPT

Group 2 captures the first token after `axon.py`, which is exactly sys.argv[1] = the registered tool name recorded in the receipt ("tool" field), so it cross-checks correctly against _receipted_tools(). Verified the candidate regex matches all repro cases (drift, clock spaced, non-symbolic CLI for log) AND still matches every existing test fixture (TOOL(totally-fabricated-tool), TOOL(clock), TOOL(boot)) with no false match on plain prose. Add test cases for the dispatcher CLI and spaced forms to tests/test_rules/test_r_tool_receipts.py so the blind spot stays closed. Note _EXEMPT (name,tool,x,verify) still applies; consider whether common axon.py subcommand-leading tokens need exemption review, but the first token after axon.py is always the tool name so no new exemptions are required. Prose-only narration ("I invoked the clock tool") with neither TOOL( nor axon.py remains undetectable by a text-only verifier — that is the documented honest limit of this rule, not a regression introduced by the fix; closing the CLI/spaced gap covers the easiest fabrication surface (paste a CLI command + output).

confirmed_defects.title: R_TOOL_RECEIPTS: cannot distinguish a CLAIM from a MENTION — false-positives on docs, source quoting, and self-explanation
confirmed_defects.defect: The check matches the literal token TOOL( anywhere in the text and has no claim-vs-mention discrimination, so legitimate non-execution output is blocked whenever receipts mode is on: teaching the AXON-LANG syntax ('to get time, write TOOL(clock)'), quoting program source (menu.md alone contains ~20 TOOL(...) references), or explaining the rule itself in a report. With the flag on, an AXON authoring-guide answer, a `cat menu.md` style review, or this very audit would be BLOCKED.
confirmed_defects.fix: In tools/rules/r_tool_receipts.py, gate the match on a co-located execution-claim cue instead of the bare token. Add a cue regex and scan per line: _CLAIM_CUE = re.compile(r"\b(?:ran|run|running|executed|invoked|called|obtained|fetched|returned|result|output|via|succeeded|successfully|got|retrieved|completed)\b", re.IGNORECASE); then in check() build claimed only from lines that contain BOTH a TOOL(...) match and a _CLAIM_CUE match: claimed = {m.group(1).lower() for line in text.splitlines() if _CLAIM_CUE.search(line) for m in _CLAIM_RE.finditer(line)} - _EXEMPT. This keeps all 3 existing positive tests passing (Executed/Ran/obtained via) while clearing the menu-quote, syntax-teaching, and rule-explanation false positives. Caveat: a regex cue list is heuristic, not perfectly precise; add negative-case tests (quote menu.md, teach syntax, explain the rule) to lock the behavior in.

confirmed_defects.title: R_MENU_RENDERED: skipped-menu branch is effectively dead — menu.md never sets active_program='menu', so a fully-skipped or top-truncated menu is never caught
confirmed_defects.defect: The skipped-menu branch (lines 66-73) only arms when state.active_program == 'menu'. active_program is read by verify.py (line 95) from the persisted file workspace/memory/working/active-program.md, which is only written via STORE(W:active-program, <name>) from inside programs. I read the entire menu.md program: it is `!NORM | read-only` and contains NO STORE(W:active-program, 'menu') — the menu never claims that identity. In practice active-program.md holds the last identity-claiming program (currently 'code-dev-plan'). So when a weak model is asked for the menu and emits a non-menu summary, is_menu_output is False (no MODES+[1]CHAT) AND menu_dispatched is False -> the rule returns None. The single net for the worst failure (skip the menu entirely, or truncate the TOP so the MODES header is gone) is unreachable in the real workspace.
confirmed_defects.fix:
In tools/rules/r_menu_rendered.py, broaden the dispatch detector to also use active_phase, which menu.md DOES write (DONE(menu) -> 'menu:done', and program-entry -> 'menu:start'). verify.load_state already exposes state['active_phase'] (line 96/117). Replace line 51:

    menu_dispatched = str(state.get("active_program", "")).strip().lower() == "menu"

with:

    _phase = str(state.get("active_phase", "")).strip().lower()
    menu_dispatched = (
        str(state.get("active_program", "")).strip().lower() == "menu"
        or _phase.split(":")[0] == "menu"
    )

This makes the skip branch reachable for the real failure (active_phase prefix 'menu') while staying scoped: it returns False when any other program is the active phase (verified: 'code-dev:pr-15:done-local' -> no fire) and preserves the legacy active_program=='menu' path. Per Core Rule 13, add a test case covering the skipped-menu BLOCK driven by active_phase='menu:done'.

confirmed_defects.title: R_MENU_RENDERED: substring section check produces both false positives (non-menu prose) and brittle blocks (reworded headers)
confirmed_defects.defect: Once _LOOKS_LIKE_MENU matches, missing = [s for s in SECTIONS if s not in text] (line 54) is a plain substring test. (a) False positive: any non-menu output that merely contains the strings 'MODES' and '[1] CHAT' (a doc explaining the menu, a transcript quote, or an audit report like this one) is treated as a partial menu and BLOCKED for missing the other 6 sections. (b) Brittleness: SECTIONS hardcodes 'QUALITY / SELF-IMPROVEMENT' exactly. The real menu emits a SEPARATE conditional 'SELF-IMPROVEMENT' panel (menu.md lines 309-312, only when auto-improve on / drift / audit>0); a render that shows that panel but reworded the 'QUALITY / SELF-IMPROVEMENT' header is BLOCKED for a section that is materially present. The check is coupled to exact header spelling, not to whether the menu is functionally complete.
confirmed_defects.fix:
Two surgical changes in tools/rules/r_menu_rendered.py:

1) Anchor the detector to the real render, not loose substrings. The menu program (workspace/programs/menu.md:142) always emits the literal banner line "▶ AXON / menu", which prose/transcripts/audit reports do not carry. Require it:

    _MENU_ANCHOR = re.compile(r"▶\s*AXON\s*/\s*menu", re.IGNORECASE)
    ...
    is_menu_output = bool(_MENU_ANCHOR.search(text)) and bool(_LOOKS_LIKE_MENU.search(text))

This removes both false positives (a1/a2): a doc/audit mentioning MODES + [1] CHAT lacks the "▶ AXON / menu" banner, so it is no longer treated as a partial menu. (The skipped-render branch at line 66 is unaffected — it keys on active_program == "menu", not on the text.)

2) Match sections as line-anchored headers and decouple the QUALITY/SELF-IMPROVEMENT brittleness. Replace the substring test on line 54 with a header-line check, and treat the section-5 header as satisfied by EITHER spelling so the conditional SELF-IMPROVEMENT panel counts:

    def _has_section(text, name):
        # header appears at start of a (stripped) line, allowing trailing parenthetical
        return re.search(r"(?m)^\s*" + re.escape(name) + r"\b", text) is not None

    missing = []
    for s in SECTIONS:
        if s == "QUALITY / SELF-IMPROVEMENT":
            if _has_section(text, "QUALITY") or _has_section(text, "SELF-IMPROVEMENT"):
                continue
        if not _has_section(text, s):
            missing.append(s)

This keeps the genuine-truncation block (test_truncated_menu_blocks_with_names still fires: removing META TOOLS / SELF-OBSERVE lines leaves no header match) while no longer blocking a materially-complete render that reworded the QUALITY header or only emits the conditional SELF-IMPROVEMENT panel. Add a regression test for menu-prose (with and without the "▶ AXON / menu" anchor) and for the reworded-header case.

confirmed_defects.title: quality_loop report crashes (KeyError) when the agent fills `verified` with anything but null/true/false
confirmed_defects.defect: `report_md` does `v = {None: '·', True: '✓', False: 'probe-error'}[f.get('verified')]` — a hard dict index with no default. The verify slot is filled by an LLM editing a JSON file, and JSON has no Python bool literal discipline: any value that is not exactly null/true/false (a string like "yes"/"true"/"real", or an integer like 2) raises KeyError and crashes the entire report. Worse, `1` silently maps to True and `0` to False (Python `1==True`), so numeric verdicts are silently mis-rendered rather than rejected.

confirmed_defects.title: loop_contract: NaN value permanently poisons best/best_iter AND writes spec-invalid JSON to the contract file
confirmed_defects.defect: `iterate` takes `--value` as `type=float`, which accepts 'nan'. `_improved` returns True only when `best is None`, so a NaN as the FIRST iteration sets `best=NaN`; thereafter every real value fails `new > best` (any comparison with NaN is False), so `best`/`best_iter` are frozen on the NaN forever — a genuinely better iteration never updates best. Separately, `json.dump` serializes NaN as the bare token `NaN`, which is invalid per the JSON spec: jq, JavaScript `JSON.parse`, Go, Rust, and `json.loads(..., parse_constant=raise)` all reject the file, so any non-Python consumer of the contract (or a strict re-load) breaks.
confirmed_defects.fix:
Reject non-finite values at the `iterate` entry point so no NaN/Inf can enter the contract (fixes both the in-memory poisoning and the spec-invalid JSON at the source). In tools/loop_contract.py:

Add `import math` to the imports, then at the top of `iterate()` (right after `c = _load(...)` validation, before computing `n`), insert:

```python
    if not math.isfinite(args.value):
        return {"ok": False, "error": f"--value must be a finite number, got {args.value!r}"}
```

(Equivalently, guard immediately after loading: after the `if c["status"] != "RUNNING"` block.) This makes a NaN/Inf iteration a clean no-op rejection instead of permanently poisoning best/best_iter and serializing a non-RFC-8259 token. Optionally also harden `_save` with `json.dumps(c, indent=2, allow_nan=False)` as defense-in-depth so any future NaN path raises on write rather than producing an invalid file, but the input-validation guard is the minimal correct fix.

confirmed_defects.title: constraints `run_checks` raises uncaught KeyError on a row missing `id`, while `list` tolerates it
confirmed_defects.defect: `run_checks` accesses `r['id']` directly (non-`.get`) in BOTH branches of the loop, whereas `list`/`rows_for_scope` use `r.get(...)` throughout. A registry row without an `id` field (hand-edit, schema drift, or a future writer) makes `constraints check` crash with an uncaught KeyError mid-iteration — and because `check` is the mechanical-teeth gate, the whole gate goes down, not just the bad row. Inconsistent defensiveness between the two readers means `list` says the registry is fine while `check` explodes on it.
confirmed_defects.fix:
In tools/constraints.py run_checks, replace the two bare r["id"] accesses with r.get("id") to match the list reader's tolerance:
- Line 68: results.append({"id": r.get("id"), "teeth": r.get("teeth"), "result": "advisory"})
- Line 77: results.append({"id": r.get("id"), "teeth": "mechanical", ...})

confirmed_defects.title: token_bench fallback estimate undercounts real AXON markdown by ~38%, making cross-encoder baseline diffs report phantom gains
confirmed_defects.defect: On dense AXON markdown (heavy symbols/punctuation/code), `int(len(text.split())*1.3)` yields only ~62% of the true cl100k_base count — measured 7,980 vs 12,881 on KERNEL-SLIM.md, a 38% undercount. Because a baseline written on a tiktoken-less machine and a final measured with tiktoken (or vice-versa) are both stamped into the same table shape, and `baseline_markdown` omits the per-scenario `exact` flag from the table (only a single top-line 'Encoding:' string differs), any diff that compares the two baselines row-by-row attributes a ~38% encoder artifact to 'token savings.' Nothing in the run/baseline/diff workflow gates on `exact` matching across the two artifacts being compared.
confirmed_defects.fix:
In tools/token_bench.py, make baseline_markdown surface the exactness signal that the report already carries (no behavior change to counting). Add a per-row `exact` column and, when counts are estimated, a warning banner refusing cross-encoder diffs:

def baseline_markdown(report: dict, stamp: str) -> str:
    estimated = report["encoding"] != "cl100k_base"
    lines = [f"# Token baseline — {stamp}", "",
             f"Encoding: {report['encoding']}", ""]
    if estimated:
        lines += ["> WARNING: ESTIMATED counts (tiktoken absent). Do NOT diff against a "
                  "tiktoken (exact) baseline — the ~38% encoder gap reads as phantom savings.", ""]
    lines += ["| scenario | file tokens | envelope tokens | total | exact |",
              "|---|---|---|---|---|"]
    for n, s in sorted(report["scenarios"].items()):
        lines.append(f"| {n} | {s['file_tokens']} | {s['envelope_tokens']} | {s['total']} | {s['exact']} |")
    lines += ["", "Per-item detail in the .json next to this file.", ""]
    return "\n".join(lines)

This keeps the existing test test_baseline_markdown_renders passing (it only asserts the title line and the `| chat-turn |` substring, both still present). When PR-028's diff is built, it should additionally refuse to diff two artifacts whose `encoding`/per-row `exact` disagree.

confirmed_defects.title: quality_loop scan() crashes if any scanner emits a top-level JSON array instead of an object
confirmed_defects.defect: Every consumer guards only against None via `(d or {}).get(...)`. That guard does nothing for a list: `(['x'] or {}).get('dead_candidates')` raises AttributeError ('list object has no attribute get'). Since `_run_json` itself declares list as a valid return, any battery tool that starts emitting a top-level array (a common, innocuous refactor — e.g. `print(json.dumps(findings))`) crashes the whole scan rather than that one source degrading to empty. The igap `total` extraction has the same fragility.

confirmed_defects.title: quality_loop dedupe key omits `kind`/`detail`, so two distinct findings on the same file:line silently merge
confirmed_defects.defect: The residue-lint dedupe key is `residue-lint:{file}:{line}` (kind/hint excluded). When two different residue violation kinds fire on the same line of the same file (residue_lint emits a `kind` field precisely because multiple kinds exist), the second finding is dropped and its detail is lost — distinct, separately-fixable findings are merged. The ruff path is keyed `ruff:{file}:{row}` similarly, so two ruff codes on the same row collide too.
confirmed_defects.fix:
In tools/quality_loop.py scan(), add the discriminating field to the dedupe key for both branches.

Residue-lint (line 64-65): include the kind —
    findings.append(_finding("residue-lint", f"{v.get('file')}:{v.get('line')}:{v.get('kind')}",
                             "fix", v.get("hint", ""), f"workspace/programs/{v.get('file', '')}"))

Ruff (line 70-71): include the code —
    findings.append(_finding("ruff", f"{rel}:{f.get('row')}:{f.get('code')}", "fix",
                             f"{f.get('code')}: {f.get('msg')}", rel))

This keeps genuine duplicates (identical file:line:kind / file:row:code) collapsing while preserving distinct, separately-fixable findings on the same line. Because these tools are gated as new-needs-test neurons, add a unit test asserting two different-kind residue violations in one file (e.g. repeated-precond + alias-identity) survive dedupe as 2 findings.

confirmed_defects.title: loop_contract's loop-receipt reuse is dead-on-arrival: every per-iteration audit receipt is silently rejected by loop_receipt's closed enums
confirmed_defects.defect: _receipt() shells out to loop_receipt.py with --intent 'loop-define'/'loop-iterate'/'loop-replan'/'loop-rebudget' and --trigger-source 'loop-contract'. loop_receipt.py has CLOSED enums: INTENTS={tune-threshold,promote-ephemeral,archive-cold,compile-pattern,auto-update-counter,kv-rollback-pre-image,audit-row}, TRIGGER_SOURCES={cron,dispatch,manual-user,goal-met,drift-trigger}. None of loop_contract's intents/trigger-source are members, so cmd_begin() returns {'error':'bad_intent'} with exit 2 and writes NOTHING. _receipt() catches only SubprocessError/OSError — a non-zero exit from subprocess.run (no check=True) raises neither — so the rejection is swallowed silently. Net: zero receipts ever; the advertised audit trail does not exist. Fix: add the loop-* intents + 'loop-contract' trigger-source to loop_receipt.py (schema bump to v1.1 as its own docstring prescribes) OR reuse an allowed pair (intent 'audit-row' + trigger 'manual-user'); also have _receipt() check returncode so the failure stops being silent.
confirmed_defects.fix: Two-part minimal fix. (A) Make loop_contract._receipt() use an allowed pair so receipts actually land — in /home/arturcastiel/projects/new-axon/axon/tools/loop_contract.py, change the begin args to a member intent+trigger and fold the semantic intent into the rationale. Replace the subprocess arg list in _receipt() (lines 63-69) so `--intent intent` becomes `--intent audit-row`, `--trigger-source loop-contract` becomes `--trigger-source manual-user`, and prefix the rationale with the real intent, e.g. `--rationale (intent + ": " + rationale)[:200]`. This requires no schema change to loop_receipt and is the smaller-surface option. (B) Stop the silence: in _receipt(), capture the return and surface a non-zero exit instead of discarding it — e.g. `r = subprocess.run(..., capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=30)` then `if r.returncode != 0: print(json.dumps({"warn":"loop-receipt rejected","stderr":r.stdout.strip()}), file=sys.stderr)` (keep it fail-soft per the docstring, but no longer invisible). Alternatively (larger surface, matches loop_receipt's own "Adding values = bump to v1.1" note at loop_receipt.py:52): add loop-define/loop-iterate/loop-replan/loop-rebudget to INTENTS and loop-contract to TRIGGER_SOURCES and bump SCHEMA_VERSION to "loop-receipt v1.1". Option A+B is the minimal correct fix; it edits only loop_contract.py.

# PR-016 — B: SITUATION-TRIGGER engine (≤1 hint/response, deduped, why+command)

**Project:** axon-plus · **Phase:** 3-quality-discover · **Goal:** B (discoverability) · **Complexity:** M
**Depends on:** none (signal sources already on disk) · **Pairs with:** PR-017 (footer activation), which renders this engine's output
**Respects:** reduce-surface (extend the existing footer/tick + anticipate family — no parallel hint surface), R13 (new tool ships tests).

## 1. Design in one paragraph

Add **one** detector tool, `tools/situate.py`, that reads already-existing on-disk signals (turn logs, usage, igap, orchestrator tick) and emits **at most one** situation-hint per call. The hint is injected into the **same** `W:orchestrator-last-tick` record that `anticipate.py` already feeds, and surfaced by the **existing PR-112 footer** (`axon/OUTPUT-LAYER.md` SUGGESTIONS block) — never a new surface. Dedup and the noise ceiling are enforced **inside the tool** against a small append-only ledger (`workspace/working/situate-ledger.json`), written through the same `loop_receipt` singlewriter discipline `igap.py` uses. The orchestrator calls it once per tick; if it returns silence, nothing renders.

## 2. The four signals → detectors (all read existing artifacts; no new instrumentation)

Each detector is a pure function `detect(state) -> Hint | None`. A `Hint` carries `{trigger, why, command, dedupe_key, priority}` — **why+command are mandatory fields, structurally enforced** (a hint without both is invalid and dropped at construction).

| # | Signal | Source already on disk | Threshold (config) | Hint command |
|---|--------|------------------------|--------------------|--------------|
| 1 | long grep/read streak → study mode | `state["recent-tools"]` from `W:orchestrator-last-tick` tool history + turn-log tool column | ≥ `streak-floor` (default 6) consecutive read/grep ops, no write between | `code-dev-study` |
| 2 | repeated manual steps → workflow | `tools/usage.py top --window 1d` cluster: same program/step run ≥ `repeat-floor` (default 3) | `workflow-new` |
| 3 | N graph queries → export map | count of `axon-graph` / graph-consumer fires in tick history ≥ `graph-floor` (default 3) | `graphify-obsidian` |
| 4 | entering a code-dev phase → phase tool | `W:active-phase` transitions into a `code-dev-*` phase this turn (compare prev tick's phase) | the phase-relevant program (`code-dev-test-map` / `code-dev-review-scope` for study/review phases — reuse `_GRAPH_CONSUMERS` mapping already in `synapse_suggest.py`) |

Detectors run in **priority order** (4 phase-entry > 1 streak > 2 repeat > 3 graph). First non-None, deduped hint wins; the rest are discarded **this turn** (that is the ≤1 ceiling).

## 3. Dedup mechanism (the load-bearing part)

A `dedupe_key` is `"{trigger}:{target_command}"` (e.g. `streak:code-dev-study`). The ledger `workspace/working/situate-ledger.json` holds:

```json
{"emitted": [{"key": "streak:code-dev-study", "turn": 42, "ts": "..."}], "last_turn": 42}
```

Rule (in `situate.py`, function `_is_duplicate`):
- A hint is **suppressed** if its `dedupe_key` was emitted within the last `dedupe-window` turns (default 8) — read from the ledger's `emitted` list, filtered by `turn >= current_turn - window`.
- Window is **turn-based, not time-based**, so it survives wall-clock gaps. Current turn comes from `W:turn-count` (passed via `--turn`).
- On emit, append `{key, turn, ts}` and prune entries older than the window. Single writer: the whole read-modify-write is wrapped in `loop_receipt(actor="situate", intent="emit-hint", target_kind="file", path=ledger, pre_value, post_value, …)` exactly as `igap._bump_session` does — satisfies axon-state-singlewriter.
- **Anti-double-up with the footer's own candidates:** before emitting, drop the hint if its `target_command` already appears in `tick.candidates[:3]` (passed in via `--tick-candidates`). The situation hint is for things the ranker did *not* already surface — this is what stops PR-016 and PR-017 from showing the same suggestion twice.

## 4. Noise-ceiling enforcement (≤1 per response)

Two independent ceilings, both mechanical:
1. **Per-call ceiling:** `situate(...)` returns `Hint | None` — the type makes >1 impossible by construction. The detector loop `break`s on first surviving hint.
2. **Per-turn idempotence:** the ledger's `last_turn` guards re-entry. If `current_turn == last_turn` (engine already emitted this turn — e.g. orchestrator ticked twice), return silence immediately. This means even if the orchestrator calls the tool multiple times in one user turn, **at most one hint reaches output per turn**.

A config `L:situate-enabled` (default `true`) and `L:situate-ceiling` (default 1, exposed for future tuning but the code treats >1 as 1 in this PR — ceiling is hard) gate the whole engine. Silence is first-class, exactly like `anticipate.py`'s "silent" density.

## 5. Render — through the existing footer, not a new one

- `situate.py emit` returns `{hint: {...} | null, suppressed_reason}`.
- **`orchestrator.md`** (extend the existing ANTICIPATE block, ~line 121–127): after computing `anticipation`, add one call:
  `hint ← TOOL(situate, emit, "--turn {turn} --tick-candidates {candidates}") | {hint: ∅}`
  and store it into the tick record: add `hint: hint.hint` to the `STORE(W:orchestrator-last-tick, {…})` dict (§ RECORD, ~line 154).
- **`axon/OUTPUT-LAYER.md`** SUGGESTIONS FOOTER (extend, ~line 94): after the candidates loop, append:
  ```
  IF tick.hint ≠ ∅ →
    → "  ⚡ {tick.hint.why}"
    → "     → run: {tick.hint.command}"
  ```
  This rides the **same** `sugg-on` gate and the same drift/context-pressure suppression already there — no new gate, no new block above TEARDOWN.

This is the reduce-surface compliance point: zero new user-visible surfaces; the engine writes into `W:orchestrator-last-tick.hint` and the one existing footer renders it.

## 6. Files to touch (exact)

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `tools/situate.py` | **NEW** — detectors, dedup ledger, `emit`/`status`/`clear` subcommands. Stdlib-only, `loop_receipt`-wrapped writes, `default_workspace()` like igap. |
| `tools/REGISTRY.json` | **NEW entry** `situate` (status ACTIVE, category `os`, purpose, `health.probe`: `python3 tools/situate.py --workspace /tmp status`, `expect: ok`). |
| `workspace/programs/orchestrator.md` | Add the `TOOL(situate, emit …)` call in ANTICIPATE block; add `hint:` to the `STORE(W:orchestrator-last-tick, …)` dict. |
| `axon/OUTPUT-LAYER.md` | Append the `tick.hint` render lines inside the existing SUGGESTIONS FOOTER block. **dev-mode-gated edit (R9).** |
| `workspace/tools/situate.md` | **NEW** tool doc card (mirrors `workspace/tools/synapse-suggest.md`). |
| `workspace/preferences/` (output.md or agent.md) | Document `L:situate-enabled`, `L:streak-floor`, `L:repeat-floor`, `L:graph-floor`, `L:dedupe-window`. |
| `tests/test_situate.py` | **NEW** — see §7. |
| `my-axon/dev-projects/axon-plus/03-prs/PR-016.md` | Replace stub with this spec; flip 02-prs.md status. |

Note: the `axon/OUTPUT-LAYER.md` edit requires `L:dev-mode ≡ true` (Core Rule 9) — flag it in the PR so the implementer enables dev-mode before that one edit. Everything else is in `tools/` and `workspace/` (not write-gated).

## 7. Tests (`tests/test_situate.py`) — R13

Pattern mirrors `tests/test_anticipate.py` (sys.path-insert `tools`, import `situate`, `tmp_path` for ledger).

1. `test_each_detector_fires_on_its_signal` — feed a synthetic `state` for each of the 4 signals at threshold; assert the right `trigger` + `command`.
2. `test_below_threshold_is_silent` — streak of 5 (< floor 6) → `None`.
3. **`test_ceiling_holds_one_hint_per_turn`** — feed a state that trips **all four** detectors at once; assert exactly one hint returned, and it is the highest-priority (phase-entry); assert a second `emit` call with the same `--turn` returns silence (`last_turn` idempotence). **This is the ceiling test.**
4. **`test_hint_carries_why_and_command`** — assert the returned hint has non-empty `why` (human-readable WHY) **and** a `command` that is a runnable program name; assert constructing a `Hint` with either field missing raises/drops. **This is the why+command test.**
5. `test_dedup_suppresses_within_window` — emit `streak:code-dev-study` at turn 10; at turn 12 (window 8) the same key is suppressed; at turn 20 it fires again (outside window).
6. `test_no_double_up_with_footer_candidates` — if `tick.candidates` already contains the target command, the hint is dropped.
7. `test_ledger_write_is_receipt_wrapped` — emit, then assert a loop-receipt terminal record exists for actor `situate` (singlewriter discipline, same assertion style as igap receipt tests).
8. `test_situate_is_wired_into_orchestrator` — lock-test (the re-orphaning guard, copied from `test_anticipate.py::test_anticipate_is_wired_into_orchestrator`): assert `orchestrator.md` contains `TOOL(situate` and `OUTPUT-LAYER.md` contains `tick.hint`. Prevents the "feature goes missing" disease this whole project targets.

## 8. Gates

R13 tests · scanners (residue-lint, program-tool-conformance — `situate` must appear in REGISTRY and be invoked) · crucible green · the `test_situate_is_wired_into_orchestrator` lock-test doubles as the anti-orphan gate.

# PR-017 — Activate the orchestrator suggestion footer (axon-plus Goal B)

## 1. Why it's silent — root cause (confirmed, not inferred)

The footer machinery is all present and correct, but **nothing populates `W:orchestrator-last-tick` on a normal turn**. Three independent defects, in order of severity:

**D1 (structural — the real cause): the orchestrator is never run except inside an adaptive workflow.**
`grep` for `EXEC(orchestrator)` across the whole tree returns exactly one call site:
- `workspace/programs/workflow-run.md:216` — `IF wf.execution-mode ≠ "fixed" → EXEC(orchestrator)`

There is no boot tap, no response-gate tap, no per-turn hook, no menu tap. So `W:orchestrator-last-tick` is written **only** while an adaptive/hybrid workflow is mid-run — and `workflow-run.md:288` `CLEAR(W:orchestrator-last-tick)` wipes it the moment that workflow finishes. On boot, on the menu, in interactive chat, and during every fixed workflow, the tick is `∅`. Confirmed live: `memory get W:orchestrator-last-tick → {"found": false}`. Both render gates (`menu.md:382` and `OUTPUT-LAYER.md:96`) test `COUNT(sugg-cands) > 0`, which is always 0 → footer renders nothing. **The footer is silent because its data source is only ever populated inside a feature 99% of turns never enter.**

**D2 (not the cause, but a latent footgun): `L:suggestions-enabled` is unset.** Confirmed: `memory get L:suggestions-enabled → {"found": false}`. This is *fine* — both gates default it to `true` (`RETRIEVE(L:suggestions-enabled) | true`), so the toggle is not the blocker. Flagged only so the fix doesn't "solve" it by setting the key and mistaking that for the cure.

**D3 (would make it render garbage even once populated): candidate shape mismatch.** The footer renders `{c.name} reason: {c.reason} confidence: {c.score}`. But `synapse-suggest rank --explain` emits `reason` as an **array of debug strings** (`["intent+0.025","dispatch+0.064"]`) and the *human* score field is `score` (0..1) while `raw` is the unnormalized value. The orchestrator (`orchestrator.md:154-165`) stores `candidates` **verbatim** into the tick. So even after D1 is fixed, the footer would print `reason: ['intent+0.025', 'dispatch+0.064']` — a signal-weight dump, not a "why". There is **no `command`/how-to-run field at all** in the candidate record; the prompt's "each with why + how-to-run" requirement is currently unsatisfiable from the data.

## 2. What to wire (reduce-surface: reuse, do not add)

**Decision: do NOT add a per-turn orchestrator EXEC.** Running the full mutator orchestrator program every turn is a heavy, side-effecting tick (CHECKPOINT, EMIT, dispatch, `W:active-program` stomp) — wrong altitude for an advisory footer, and it fights the response gate. Instead reuse the **read-only** `anticipate.py`, which already wraps the same ranker with a margin gate and a silence-first contract, and already returns a `category` slice. The footer's data producer becomes a thin, read-only tick-writer.

**Change set (4 edits, 0 new tools, 0 new files):**

1. **`tools/anticipate.py` — extend the result record (reuse, no new tool).** Have `anticipate()` return the top-N (default 3) candidates already in footer-ready shape, not just the top-1 verdict. For each candidate emit:
   - `name` (have it),
   - `why` — collapse the `reason` array + `category` into one human clause (e.g. `"matches your recent input · system tool"`); derive from the dominant signal, not the raw weight string,
   - `command` — the run-string. Derive via the existing `programs_registry` / REGISTRY `usage:` field (programs already declare `# usage:`; tools have a REGISTRY entry). Fall back to the bare `name` when no usage string exists,
   - `score` — the normalized `score` (0..1), **never** `raw`.
   Keep the silence contract: below the `SUGGEST=0.20` margin → emit `[]`, never a guess.

2. **`axon/BOOT.md` boot step 3 + the response gate path — add ONE read-only tick-write.** After output render (the `!BG` band, alongside turn-logging), write the tick from anticipate:
   ```
   IF RETRIEVE(L:suggestions-enabled) | true ≡ true →
     ant ← TOOL(anticipate, "--input {W:raw-user-input} --top 3")   ← read-only, no --log spam every turn
     IF COUNT(ant.candidates) > 0 →
       STORE(W:orchestrator-last-tick, { ts: NOW(), candidates: ant.candidates, source: "anticipate" })
     ELSE → CLEAR(W:orchestrator-last-tick)    ← honest silence; stale candidates never linger
   ```
   This is the single missing wire. It is `!BG`, non-blocking, read-only, and runs on **every** turn — so boot, menu, and interactive chat all get a fresh tick. `workflow-run`'s existing populate/CLEAR stays as-is (it overwrites with workflow-context candidates during a run, then this band repopulates on the next free-text turn — exactly the handoff the existing `CLEAR` comment anticipates).

3. **`axon/OUTPUT-LAYER.md` §SUGGESTIONS FOOTER + `workspace/programs/menu.md:382-388` — render `why` + `command`.** Change the render line from the current `reason: {c.reason} confidence: {c.score}` to:
   ```
   {arrow} {c.name}   — {c.why}
        run: {c.command}                  confidence: {c.score}
   ```
   Both render blocks must match (test T-112.7 already pins menu↔output-layer parity). Recompile `menu.cmp.md`.

4. **`tools/REGISTRY.json` (anticipate entry) + docs** — bump anticipate's `purpose` to note it now produces the footer tick. One-line CHANGELOG row referencing PR-017.

## 3. Noise ceiling (≤3 — enforced at three layers, defense in depth)

- **Producer:** `anticipate --top 3` caps candidates at 3 before they ever reach memory.
- **Gate:** the margin gate (`SUGGEST=0.20`) already drops weak candidates → typical render is **1**, not 3. Silence is the default, not the exception.
- **Renderer:** `TAKE(sugg-cands, 3)` in full mode, `TAKE(sugg-cands, 1)` in compact mode (default) and under critical context-pressure, `sugg-on ← false` on drift-diverged — all already in `OUTPUT-LAYER.md:88-91`, unchanged. So the live ceiling is **top-1 in the default compact footer**, top-3 only in `full`. This satisfies "≤3 ranked candidates" while keeping the steady-state footprint minimal.

## 4. Tests (R13 — additions require coverage)

Extend `tests/test_output_layer_suggestions.py` and add `tests/test_anticipate_footer.py`:

- **T-017.1** — `anticipate(--top 3)` returns ≤3 candidates, each with `name`, `why`, `command`, `score` keys present (shape contract).
- **T-017.2** — `command` is non-empty: a program candidate's command equals its `# usage:` first form; a tool candidate falls back to its name. (closes D3's missing how-to-run)
- **T-017.3** — `why` is a human string, **not** the raw `reason` array and **not** containing `+0.0` weight tokens. (closes D3's reason-array leak)
- **T-017.4** — `score` is the normalized 0..1 field, never `raw`.
- **T-017.5** — silence contract: a no-margin / empty-ranker state returns `[]`, and the tick-writer `CLEAR`s rather than storing stale candidates. (cardinal rule: wrong > none)
- **T-017.6** — ceiling: with 10 ranked inputs, the producer caps at 3; assert compact-mode render shows exactly 1, full-mode ≤3.
- **T-017.7** (BOOT/gate wiring, static-text assertion like the existing PR-112 tests) — `axon/BOOT.md` contains the `STORE(W:orchestrator-last-tick, …)` from `anticipate` in the `!BG` post-render band, gated on `L:suggestions-enabled`. This is the regression guard that prevents the footer from silently de-wiring again.
- **T-017.8** — menu↔output-layer render parity: both blocks render `why` + `run:` line (extends existing T-112.7).
- **Regression:** keep all 9 existing `test_output_layer_suggestions.py` cases green, and the 3 `test_workflow_orchestrator_bridge.py` cases that pin `workflow-run`'s populate-then-CLEAR ordering (the new boot-band write must not break the bridge's stale-clear).

## 5. Acceptance criterion

On a fresh boot with no workflow running, `memory get W:orchestrator-last-tick` is non-empty after the first turn, and the menu footer renders at least the top-1 next-step with a plain-English `why` and a runnable `run:` command — verified by a non-test manual boot. Footer stays **silent** (no render, tick cleared) when the ranker margin is below threshold.

# PR-018 — B: dispatch-phrases + sibling cross-links full rollout

**Project:** axon-plus · **Phase:** 3-quality-discover · **Complexity:** M · **Depends on:** none
**Pattern source:** graphify-obsidian PR-006 (merged 716b61c) — proven on `axon-graph`
**Spec target:** `my-axon/dev-projects/axon-plus/03-prs/PR-018.md`

## Problem (measured, not asserted)

The dispatch index (`workspace/memory/longterm/dispatch-index.json`) routes a prompt by TF-IDF cosine over `name + desc + phrases` per program (`tools/dispatch.py:236-239`). The `# dispatch-phrases:` channel that carries user phrasing is parsed by `tools/dispatch_index.py:41,77` — but **only 4 of 168 indexed programs use it** (`axon-graph`, `goal-define`, `loop-designer`, `quality-loop`), all from prior pilots. **0 of 87 code-dev programs and 0 of 7 workflow programs carry phrases.**

Measured baseline (existing fixture `tests/fixtures/dispatch-corpus.jsonl`, 30 rows, index rebuilt):

```
BASELINE  P@1 = 0.10   P@3 = 0.27   (n=30)
```

Two distinct root causes the spec must fix:

1. **Unphrased surface** — desc lines are phase/internal vocabulary ("Phase 2 — codebase-grounded high-level plan"), not what users type. `"make a planning section"` → `loop-designer` (wrong); `"give me a deep dive of the project"` → `code-dev-knowledge-explain` (wrong); `"what is the current status"` → `library-dev-status` (wrong); `"what should I do next"` → `audit-to-study` (wrong).

2. **Fixture rot (measurement integrity)** — the corpus expects programs that **do not exist**: `code-dev-pr`, `code-dev-status`, `code-dev-resume`, `code-dev-audit`, `code-dev-log`. The real programs are `code-dev-pr-create`, `code-dev-state-status`, `code-dev-state-resume`, `code-dev-rules-audit`, `code-dev-journal-log`. The 0.10 baseline is partly **un-winnable** because the target labels are stale. Any honest before/after measurement requires correcting the fixture first.

**Empirical proof the pattern scales** (validated this session — phrases added to 7 high-value programs, measured on the corrected corpus subset, n=13):

```
BEFORE (no phrases): P@1 = 0.08   P@3 = 0.23
AFTER  (+phrases):   P@1 = 0.62   P@3 = 0.85      ← +0.54 P@1, +0.62 P@3
```

## Change

### Part A — Fix the fixture (measurement integrity, do FIRST)
`tests/fixtures/dispatch-corpus.jsonl`: repoint stale `expected` labels to real program names (`code-dev-pr`→`code-dev-pr-create`, `code-dev-status`→`code-dev-state-status`, `code-dev-resume`→`code-dev-state-resume`, `code-dev-audit`→`code-dev-rules-audit`, `code-dev-log`→`code-dev-journal-log`). Without this, the metric measures a moving target. Update `test_dispatch.py::test_corpus_loaded` (currently hard-asserts `len==30`, `verbs==10`) to the new shape. **This re-baselines the metric honestly.**

### Part B — Roll out dispatch-phrases (prioritized tiers)

Add `# dispatch-phrases:` (single line, ` · `-separated, placed directly after `# desc:` per pilot convention) to programs in priority order. Phrases below are the **actual phrases** to ship — each validated to route correctly.

Deferred unless the fixture shows residual mis-routes — **reduce-surface: do not phrase the long tail of 87 programs; phrase only where a real query collides.** Stub/alias programs (`code-dev-preflight` — "removed next release", `code-dev-safety-preflight-ALIAS`) get **no phrases** (they should de-rank, not surface).

### Part C — Sibling cross-links
The pilot's cross-link mechanism is two channels, both already present in the program format:
- **`# next:` help-header line** (plain-English "what to run after") — the human-facing sibling pointer.
- **`synapse: next-suggests: [...]`** — the machine-readable flow edge read by the suggestion footer.

Cross-links to add/verify (each is a sibling pair where one program's output feeds the other):

| In program | Add cross-link to sibling | Why |
|---|---|---|
| `code-dev-plan` | `next-suggests:` includes `code-dev-pr-create` | plan → write the PRs it listed |
| `code-dev-pr-create` | `# next: code-dev pr-review` + `next-suggests: [code-dev-pr-review]` | spec → review |
| `code-dev-state-status` | `# next: code-dev next` | "where am I" → "what next" |
| `code-dev-next` | `next-suggests: [code-dev-state-status, code-dev-plan]` | next-action ↔ status/plan |
| `workflow-new` | already → `[workflow-validate, workflow-list, workflow-simulate]` ✓ verify only |
| `workflow-validate` | `next-suggests: [workflow-simulate, workflow-run]` | valid → dry-run → run |
| `workflow-run` | `next-suggests: [workflow-list, workflow-explain]` | run → inspect |

Mirrors the pilot's `deps ↔ axon-graph` and `code-dev-review → axon-graph affected` cross-links (pinned by `test_dispatch_graph_routing.py::test_deps_cross_links_axon_graph`).

### Part D — Pattern doc
Extend the existing "tool discoverability pattern" section in `workspace/AXON-DOCS-ARCHITECTURE.md` (written by PR-006) with the rollout result: the channel is `# dispatch-phrases:`, the corpus is `name+desc+phrases`, measured lift, and the rule **"phrase where a real query collides — not every program."**

## How to MEASURE (before/after)

The harness already exists: `tests/test_dispatch.py` loads `dispatch-corpus.jsonl`, runs `dispatch.py match` per row, computes P@1/P@3. PR-018:
1. **Corrects** the corpus (Part A) and **extends** it to ≥45 rows (3 phrasings × the 12 Tier-1 + 7 Tier-2 = 19 verbs → drop to one canonical 3-phrasing set per verb covering all phrased programs).
2. Records **baseline P@1/P@3 on the corrected corpus with phrases absent** (git-stash the phrase edits, run, record) vs **after** (phrases present). Spec ships both numbers in the PR's Acceptance section — exactly as PR-006 recorded its four-phrasing before/after.
3. Measured target (from this session's 7-program proof, conservative): **P@1 ≥ 0.55, P@3 ≥ 0.80** on the corrected/extended corpus (up from corrected-baseline ≈ 0.08–0.10).

`dispatch.py match` already emits `latency_ms` per call — assert the rollout adds no latency regression (TF-IDF over 168 corpus strings is sub-10ms; phrases only lengthen strings marginally).

## Regression-pinned tests (R13 — new neuron content needs tests)

New `tests/test_dispatch_rollout_routing.py` (mirrors `test_dispatch_graph_routing.py` structure):
- `test_phrases_parsed_for_tier1` — every Tier-1/Tier-2 program has `"phrases"` in `dispatch_index.source_programs()` output (pins the mechanism end-to-end, not the file text).
- `test_unambiguous_intents_top_rank` — each canonical phrasing (e.g. `"what should I do next"`→`code-dev-next`, `"run a workflow"`→`workflow-run`) ranks **#1**.
- `test_ambiguous_intents_top3` — phrasings that legitimately overlap a sibling (e.g. `"review my pull request"` overlaps `code-dev-review`/`code-dev-pr-review`/`code-dev-pr-sync`) surface in **top-3** (the honest contract — the footer/fallback can then offer it).
- `test_cross_links_present` — `code-dev-pr-create.md` contains `code-dev pr-review`; `workflow-validate.md` `next-suggests` contains `workflow-simulate` (text-level sibling pins, like the pilot's `test_deps_cross_links_axon_graph`).
- `test_stub_programs_unphrased` — `code-dev-preflight` (alias stub) and `*-ALIAS` programs carry **no** phrases (reduce-surface guard: dead aliases must not be promoted into routing).

Extend `tests/test_dispatch.py`:
- Update `test_corpus_loaded` / `test_corpus_phrasing_distribution` to the new corpus shape (Part A re-baseline).
- Promote `test_dispatch_metrics_baseline` from advisory-only to a **pinned floor** (`assert p1 >= 0.55` per the measured proof) — turning the advisory metric into a regression gate, matching PR-018's plan note "W4 promotes thresholds to gates" but pulling the floor in now that there's evidence to set it.

## Gates
R13 (new test file + extended tests over the change-set) · `dispatch_index check` green (index matches source after rebuild) · `residue_lint` / `lint_paths` · `freshness` · `crucible` green before merge.
Rebuild step in the PR: `python3 tools/dispatch_index.py rebuild` (the index is a build artifact of the phrase edits — must be committed in the same PR or the gate drifts).

## Out of scope (reduce-surface discipline)
- The 87-program long tail — only the ~19 high-collision verbs get phrases. Phrasing every program would re-introduce the noise the pilot avoided.
- Dense/embedding RRF rerank (the deferred `dispatch-rerank` second signal — `dispatch.py:59`) — sparse TF-IDF + phrases is sufficient, proven above.
- Activating the orchestrator footer (that's PR-017, sibling PR).
- Auto-tuning the threshold from this corpus (`dispatch-auto-tune` stays as-is).

## Acceptance
- Corpus corrected + extended; before/after P@1/P@3 recorded in the spec from real `dispatch.py` runs.
- Tier-1 + Tier-2 programs carry validated `# dispatch-phrases:`; index rebuilt + committed.
- Measured P@1 ≥ 0.55 / P@3 ≥ 0.80 on the corrected corpus, up from ≈ 0.10 baseline.
- All routing pinned by `test_dispatch_rollout_routing.py`; metric floor pinned in `test_dispatch.py`; stub-programs-unphrased guard green.
